title: Statistics Basics — Describing Data with Numbers
key_insight: Before building any model, you must understand your data. A handful of numbers — mean, median, standard deviation — tell you the most important things about a dataset's centre and spread.
body:
## Measures of Centre

```python
scores = [72, 85, 90, 68, 95, 88, 72, 79, 95, 61]

# Mean: average value
mean = sum(scores) / len(scores)
print(f'Mean: {mean}')  # 80.5

# Or use statistics module:
import statistics
print(statistics.mean(scores))    # 80.5
print(statistics.median(scores))  # 82.0 — middle value (less affected by outliers)
print(statistics.mode(scores))    # 72 — most frequent value
```

**Mean vs Median — when they differ:**
- `[1, 2, 3, 4, 1000]` → mean = 202, median = 3
- One extreme outlier pulls the mean far from most values
- **Use median for skewed data** (income, house prices, response times)
- **Use mean for symmetric data**

## Measures of Spread

```python
scores = [72, 85, 90, 68, 95, 88, 72, 79, 95, 61]

# Range: difference between max and min
print(max(scores) - min(scores))  # 34

# Standard deviation: average distance from the mean
print(statistics.stdev(scores))   # 10.9 — most values within ~11 points of mean

# Variance: standard deviation squared
print(statistics.variance(scores)) # 119.2
```

## Why Standard Deviation Matters

```
Class A scores: [79, 80, 81, 80, 80]  → mean=80, stdev=0.7  (consistent)
Class B scores: [50, 60, 80, 90, 100] → mean=76, stdev=18.7 (spread out)
```

Same mean ≠ same performance! Standard deviation captures the story the mean misses.

## Quick Visualisation

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

scores = [72, 85, 90, 68, 95, 88, 72, 79, 95, 61]

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))

plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
plt.hist(scores, bins=6, edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Distribution of Scores')
plt.xlabel('Score')
plt.ylabel('Count')

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
plt.boxplot(scores)
plt.title('Box Plot')
plt.ylabel('Score')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
# Box plot shows: min, Q1, median, Q3, max — and outliers
```
common_mistakes: Using mean on skewed data — report median for house prices, income, response times
Ignoring the spread — two datasets can have the same mean but very different distributions
`statistics.stdev()` gives the sample standard deviation (divides by n-1); `statistics.pstdev()` gives population stdev (divides by n)
mini_exercise: Given exam scores: [45, 72, 68, 91, 38, 82, 76, 55, 94, 63, 88, 71]. Calculate: mean, median, mode, range, standard deviation. Then add an outlier (200) and recalculate mean and median. Which changes more?
real_world_usage: A/B testing at tech companies computes mean, median, and standard deviation of user metrics (time on page, click rate) for each variant. The mean alone can be misleading — a 1% improvement in mean click rate might be from 1% of users clicking 100× more. The distribution (stdev, percentiles) tells the real story.
explain_back_prompt: What is the difference between mean and median? When would you use each? What does standard deviation tell you that the mean doesn't?

build_task.title: Build a statistics summary function
build_task.context: A reusable function that summarises any numeric dataset.
steps: Write `summarise(data)` that returns a dict with: mean, median, stdev, min, max, range, count
Test with: normal data, skewed data (with outliers), and a list with only 1 value
Plot a histogram of the data
Add percentiles: 25th, 75th, and interquartile range (IQR = Q3 - Q1)
build_task.expected_output: A dict like {'mean': 72.3, 'median': 74.0, 'stdev': 12.1, ...} and a histogram.
build_task.starter_code:
import statistics
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def summarise(data):
    if len(data) < 2:
        return {'error': 'need at least 2 values'}
    # TODO: compute all statistics
    # TODO: return dict
    pass

data = [45, 72, 68, 91, 38, 82, 76, 55, 94, 63, 88, 71]
print(summarise(data))
# TODO: plot histogram
